feed.title: Shanghao Wu
feed.subtitle: Personal site of Shanghao Wu — mathematics student exploring data, modeling, and visualization.

entry.title: The Price Is Watching You
entry.content:
4

In 1985, just four things decided the price of a plane ticket.

hundreds

In 2026, it's hundreds.

And many of them are about you.

↓

It's the same plane ticket.

In 1985, four factors set its price. Season, inventory, fuel cost, and what competitors charged. All about the market.

In 2026, it's hundreds. Most of them are about you.

1985 · Market
Season Inventory Fuel cost Competitors
2026 · You

Traditional pricing typically views consumers as a collective group. Stores set a uniform price that fluctuates based on seasonality and demand. While such practices can sometimes cause consumer dissatisfaction, customers generally understand the reasons behind the price changes.

Algorithmic pricing, however, renders this process more personalized yet far less transparent. Companies leverage vast amounts of data to predict an individual consumer's willingness to pay, pushing the price as close as possible to the upper limit of what that person is willing to accept. In doing so, consumers lose certain rights that were once crucial, including the right to be informed, the ability to compare prices and make choices, and the capacity to judge whether they are being treated fairly.

So, what's your price?

The algorithm isn't deciding how much to charge you. It's deciding how much to charge someone like you. And "someone like you" is assembled from your device, location, time, and behavior. Each signal looks harmless on its own. Stacked together, they sketch a portrait, and that portrait sets the price you see.

Flip the five switches below and see how your profile can change the price.

The percentages below are illustrative, not any company's actual pricing formula. They show how small personal signals can add up to a larger price difference. Real pricing algorithms are often hidden from consumers.

Baseline price fair market price
$12.00
Your algorithmic price
$15.00
Worst-case price a portrait flagged "easy to exploit"
$34.80

What just happened?

The algorithm read five signals about you and assembled a portrait. Each one barely moves the price on its own. A premium phone, maybe +8%. Low battery, another +12%. Late at night, +6%. A fifth search, +9%.

But stacked together, the same product can cost you twice what someone else pays.

You never see this math. You only see a price.

When Your Data Becomes the Price

Algorithmic pricing does not take a little more from everyone evenly. It finds the people who cannot walk away, cannot see clearly, and cannot compare, and it charges them more. The four figures below are what that targeted harm looks like.

Data comes from the cited research, government filings, and news investigations.

In a 2016 NPR interview, Keith Chen, then Uber's head of economic research, noted that a smartphone's battery level can serve as a key indicator for predicting a user's sensitivity to surge pricing.

Users with low battery levels are generally less willing to wait for prices to drop or to look for alternatives. They are more likely to accept the higher price in front of them because they fear losing the ability to request a ride if their phone dies. The Uber platform can detect when consumers are in a rush, when they lack alternatives, and when they are less likely to reject a given price.

This is precisely what makes algorithmic pricing a cause for concern. The product itself remains unchanged. What changes is the platform's insight into the consumer's situation. Once that situation is identified through data, it can be used as a signal to estimate the consumer's willingness to pay.

Across three major Indian delivery platforms, iPhone users paid about 3% more per item on average than Android users.

It looks small. But multiplied across a year of orders, a household pays enough extra, only because it used the wrong phone, to buy 30 kilograms of rice.

RealPage's YieldStar software gives landlords rent pricing recommendations. When many landlords in the same area rely on the same algorithm, prices may no longer come only from independent competition. On the surface, landlords are not sitting together to set rents. But in practice, they may all be following the same system's advice.

For tenants, the problem is not only that rents may rise. The deeper problem is that competition becomes harder to see. Tenants may think they are comparing prices across different apartments, but those prices may be shaped by the same algorithmic logic.

Consumer surplus refers to the value retained by a consumer when the price actually paid is lower than their maximum willingness to pay. If you are willing to pay $20 but actually pay only $12, the $8 difference represents your consumer surplus.

Algorithmic pricing alters this balance. By leveraging browsing history, geographic location, device type, and other behavioral characteristics, companies can estimate the price a specific consumer is willing to pay. The closer the price comes to this psychological upper limit, the less surplus value the consumer retains.

A 2020 study by Shiller found that personalized pricing based on browsing history could boost corporate profits by approximately 13%, whereas traditional pricing based on demographic characteristics yielded an increase of only 0.25%. This additional profit does not materialize out of thin air. Rather, it stems from a transfer of value from consumers to companies.

You can't see the algorithm.
But now you can see what it costs you.

The receipt doesn't tell you the algorithm flagged your battery. It just says $22.80.

The rent contract doesn't tell you the building next door uses the same software. It just says $2,840 a month.

The plane ticket doesn't tell you it watched you search this route four times this week. It just says $489.

You were never going to see it. Now you've seen what it looks like.

Three ways to take back some control.

You may not be able to see the full algorithm, but you can still make yourself harder to profile, pause when urgency is being used against you, and make hidden price differences visible.

01

Make yourself harder to read.

Clear cookies. Use private windows. Compare prices across devices and accounts. The algorithm gets less aggressive when its picture of you is blurry.

02

Notice when you're being made to feel urgent.

Low battery. Late night. Limited availability warnings. Countdown timers. These moments can push you to accept the first price. If you can wait, wait. The price often changes.

03

Share what you just saw.

The single biggest thing protecting algorithmic pricing is that most people don't know it exists. Awareness is the first defense.

Sources

  1. Chen, M. Keith. Interview by Shankar Vedantam. "Your Brain on Uber." Hidden Brain, NPR, 17 May 2016.
  2. Chen, M. Keith, and Michael Sheldon. "Dynamic Pricing in a Labor Market: Surge Pricing and Flexible Work on the Uber Platform." Proceedings of the 2016 ACM Conference on Economics and Computation, ACM, 2016, p. 455.
  3. Federal Trade Commission. "FTC Surveillance Pricing 6(b) Study: Research Summaries — A Staff Perspective." January 2025.
  4. Kumar, Krish, et al. "Research Problem Statement." Unpublished manuscript, 2026.
  5. McBride, John. Quoted in "The smart AI behind effective airline revenue management." PhocusWire, 17 December 2024.
  6. Mone, A., et al. "AI Price Tags and Privacy: When Your Data Sets Your Price." Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery, vol. 16, no. 1, 2026, p. e70070.
  7. Shiller, Benjamin Reed. "Approximating Purchase Propensities and Reservation Prices from Broad Consumer Tracking." International Economic Review, vol. 61, no. 2, 2020, pp. 847–870.
  8. United States Department of Justice et al. v. RealPage, Inc. et al. Civil Action No. 24-cv-710, M.D.N.C., 23 August 2024.
  9. Vogell, Heather. "Rent Going Up? One Company's Algorithm Could Be Why." ProPublica, 15 October 2022.
entry.summary: How algorithms quietly redefine the relationship between you and the market.

entry.title: Hello, world.
entry.content:
Starting a blog feels like buying a new notebook — half the value is the blank page in front of you, and half is the quiet promise that you’ll show up to fill it.

I plan to write here about three kinds of things:

  • Math I’m learning — short notes, worked examples, intuition I wish someone had told me earlier.
  • Data projects — what I tried, what broke, and the chart that finally made sense.
  • Side observations — things from soccer, books, faith, anything that quietly shapes how I think.

Posts will be short more often than long. If you want to follow along, there’s an RSS feed — or just check back when you’re curious.

Thanks for being here.
entry.summary: Starting a blog feels like buying a new notebook — half the value is the blank page in front of you, and half is the quiet promise that you’ll show up to fill it.
